name: library_facewatch_request_131
premise: Marcus Fernandez, the library commissioner of Harbor Library Facewatch Request Institute, has received an urgent police request to install temporary facial-recognition cameras in the central library after a series of assaults near transit tunnels. Detectives believe the suspect is using library computers each afternoon and say the system could identify him within days. Privacy policy has long shielded patrons because undocumented residents, abuse survivors, and unhoused people rely on the library as one of the few anonymous public spaces left in the city. Civil-liberties lawyers warn that once surveillance enters the building it may not truly leave, especially if the mayor campaigns on the temporary success before the quarterly filing. Marcus Fernandez must decide whether to approve the deployment.

agents.name: Marcus Fernandez
agents.identity: I am the library commissioner of Harbor Library Facewatch Request Institute, responsible for keeping public space both safe and meaningfully free.
agents.goal: I want to prevent further violence without converting the library into an extension of real-time policing.

agents.name: Niko Alvarez
agents.identity: I am Niko Alvarez, a police captain facing families who want to know why we are refusing a tool that might stop the next assault.
agents.goal: I want the cameras approved for a limited deployment because catching a dangerous suspect quickly would protect vulnerable riders and library users.

choices: I will deny the facial-recognition request because library patrons' rights to privacy and uninhibited access should not be compromised by temporary policing demands.
I will approve a strictly time-limited facial-recognition deployment because preventing another assault justifies this targeted surveillance measure.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Marcus Fernandez must decide whether facial-recognition cameras are temporarily installed in the central library. What does Marcus Fernandez do?
